section.title: The Weak and the Strong
section.sectionRationale: Having called the church to love one another and to put on Christ, Paul now turns to a concrete threat to that love: disputes over food and holy days between believers of tender and of robust conscience. He refuses to let secondary matters divide those Christ has welcomed. The one who eats all things must not despise, the one who abstains must not judge, for each belongs to the Lord and each will stand before God's judgment seat. The chapter reframes Christian freedom under the higher law of love that will not wound a brother for whom Christ died.
section.thesis: In matters of conscience that Scripture leaves open, believers are to welcome one another rather than despise or judge, for each of us belongs to and answers to the Lord; and love will gladly limit its own liberty rather than place a stumbling block before a brother for whom Christ died.
section.frameNote: Four movements: do not judge the servant of another, for each stands or falls to his own Master; we will all give account at God's judgment seat; do not by your eating destroy a brother, for the kingdom is righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit; and act from faith, for whatever is not from faith is sin.

units.label: Welcome the weak, do not judge the Master's servant
units.note: to his own master he stands or falls

verses.text: {{proslambano|Accept}} him whose faith is {{asthenes|weak}}, without passing judgment on his opinions.
For one person has faith to eat all things, while another, who is weak, eats only vegetables.
The one who eats everything must not belittle the one who does not, and the one who does not eat everything must not judge the one who does, for God has accepted him.
Who are you to judge someone else’s servant? To his own master he stands or falls. And he will stand, for the Lord is able to make him stand.
One person regards a certain day above the others, while someone else considers every day alike. Each one should be fully convinced in his own mind.
He who observes a special day does so to the Lord; he who eats does so to the Lord, for he gives thanks to God; and he who abstains does so to the Lord and gives thanks to God.

units.label: We belong to the Lord and will give account to Him
units.note: we will all stand before God's judgment seat

verses.text: For none of us lives to himself alone, and none of us dies to himself alone.
If we live, we live to the Lord, and if we die, we die to the Lord. So whether we live or die, we belong to the Lord.
For this reason Christ died and returned to life, that He might be the Lord of both the dead and the living.
Why, then, do you {{krino|judge}} your brother? Or why do you belittle your brother? For we will all stand before God’s judgment seat.
It is written: “As surely as I live, says the Lord, every knee will bow before Me; every tongue will confess to God.”
So then, each of us will give an account of himself to God.

units.label: Do not destroy a brother for whom Christ died
units.note: the kingdom is righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit

verses.text: Therefore let us stop judging one another. Instead, make up your mind not to put any {{skandalon|stumbling block}} or obstacle in your brother’s way.
I am convinced and fully persuaded in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself. But if anyone regards something as unclean, then for him it is unclean.
If your brother is distressed by what you eat, you are no longer acting in love. Do not by your eating destroy your brother, for whom Christ died.
Do not allow what you consider good, then, to be spoken of as evil.
For the {{basileia|kingdom}} of God is not a matter of eating and drinking, but of righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.
For whoever serves Christ in this way is pleasing to God and approved by men.

units.label: Pursue peace, and act from faith
units.note: whatever is not from faith is sin

verses.text: So then, let us pursue what leads to peace and to mutual edification.
Do not destroy the work of God for the sake of food. All food is clean, but it is wrong for a man to let his eating be a stumbling block.
It is better not to eat meat or drink wine or to do anything to cause your brother to stumble.
Keep your belief about such matters between yourself and God. Blessed is the one who does not condemn himself by what he approves.
But the one who has doubts is condemned if he eats, because his eating is not from faith; and everything that is not from faith is sin.

proslambano.gloss: To receive, welcome, take to oneself. It is a warm word, used of taking someone in as a guest or a friend. Paul commands the church to welcome the believer of tender conscience fully and gladly, not on probation, because God Himself has already welcomed him. The same welcome will be grounded in chapter fifteen in the way Christ welcomed us.

asthenes.gloss: Weak, without strength, feeble. Here it describes not weak faith in Christ but a faith not yet strong enough to be free in matters of food and days; the conscience is tender and easily wounded. Paul never mocks such a believer but calls the strong to bear with him gently, since he too is one for whom Christ died.

krino.gloss: To judge, to pass verdict, to condemn. Paul forbids believers from setting themselves up as judges over a brother in disputable matters, because there is one Judge before whose seat we will all stand. To usurp God's bench is to forget that the one you judge is the Lord's servant, not yours.

skandalon.gloss: A trap or snare, the trigger stick of a snare, hence a stumbling block that causes another to fall. Love refuses to lay such a trap in a brother's path. The strong may be free to eat, but they are not free to wield that freedom in a way that wounds a weaker conscience and trips a fellow believer into sin.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, reign, the rule of God. Paul insists the kingdom of God is not measured by what we eat or drink but by righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit. To divide the church over food is to mistake the whole nature of God's reign, which is a matter of the Spirit's fruit, not of dietary rules.

basic.context: Paul turns from the great themes of the letter to a very practical problem in the Roman church. Some believers, likely with a Jewish background or scruples carried over from pagan temple practice, felt they could only eat vegetables and must keep certain holy days. Others knew they were free to eat anything. The two groups were despising and judging each other. Paul does not settle the menu; he settles the attitude. Welcome one another, because God has welcomed each of you.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter is about matters Scripture leaves open, disputable things, not the core of the gospel. Paul is not soft on truth; in Galatians he fought to the death over the gospel itself. But food and days are not the gospel, and here he pleads for patience and love. Read it as a manual for how a church holds together strong and tender consciences without either side ruining the other.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul says proslambano, welcome, the one who is asthenes, weak in faith. He forbids us to krino, to judge, a brother who is the Lord's own servant. He warns against laying a skandalon, a stumbling block, in his path. And he reminds us that the basileia, the kingdom of God, is not food and drink but righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.
basic.typology: The clean and unclean foods of the law pointed forward to a deeper cleanness. Now that Christ has come and declared all foods clean, the shadow gives way to the substance. The weak believer still living among the shadows is to be treated tenderly, for the law was a guardian until Christ, and the freedom the strong enjoy is the freedom Christ purchased.
basic.christ: Christ is Lord of the living and the dead; He died and rose again precisely to be our Master, so that whether we live or die we belong to Him. The brother we are tempted to despise is one for whom Christ died. To wound him carelessly is to trample the value Christ placed on him at the cross. Christ's lordship and Christ's death together dismantle our judging.
basic.god: God has welcomed both the strong and the weak, so who are we to reject whom He receives? He is the one Judge before whose seat every knee will bow and every tongue confess. Because God alone judges the heart, we are freed from playing judge and freed to serve one another in love. His kingdom is righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 7 records Jesus declaring all foods clean. First Corinthians 8 and 10 handle the same question of food and a weaker brother's conscience. Colossians 2 warns against letting anyone judge us over food, drink, or festivals, which were a shadow of Christ. Isaiah 45 stands behind the vow that every knee will bow.
basic.oneStory: The gospel that justifies the ungodly by faith also gathers strong and weak into one family. The clean and unclean laws that once marked Israel off have done their work and pointed to Christ. Now, in Him, the dividing line is erased, and the one story of God moves toward a kingdom where a countless people from every conscience and nation bow before the one Lord.

context.literary: After the ethical exhortations of chapters twelve and thirteen, Paul takes up a specific pastoral case that runs from 14:1 through 15:13. The unit is bracketed by the command to welcome: welcome the weak here, and welcome one another as Christ welcomed you in the next chapter. The argument moves by reasons, each introduced by for, grounding the appeal in the lordship of Christ and the coming judgment of God.
context.historical: The Roman church included Jewish believers who had returned after the expulsion under Claudius and Gentile believers who had led the church in their absence. Tensions over food laws, wine, and sabbaths and festivals were natural fault lines between these groups. Some scruples also came from the fear of meat once offered to idols. Paul writes to a mixed body learning to live together under one Lord.
context.canonical: The chapter belongs with the New Testament's teaching on Christian liberty and the weaker brother, especially First Corinthians 8 to 10. It has been a touchstone for the Reformation distinction between things commanded, things forbidden, and things indifferent, the adiaphora, in which conscience must be respected and love must rule.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To stop the mutual despising and judging in the Roman church over food and holy days, by grounding unity in the lordship of Christ and the coming judgment of God, and to call the strong to limit their liberty in love so that no weaker brother is destroyed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific issues, vegetables and special days, are examples from the first century. The abiding prescription is the principle: in disputable matters welcome one another, do not judge or despise, act from faith, and never let your freedom wound a brother for whom Christ died.

debates.question: Who are the weak in faith here?

views.label: Believers with tender consciences over food and days
views.case: The historic reading takes the weak as genuine Christians whose faith is not yet strong enough to enjoy full freedom, often shaped by Jewish scruples or fear of idol food; Paul calls them weak in this respect, not weak in saving faith.

views.label: A merely cultural or ethnic label
views.case: Some stress the Jew and Gentile background and read weak and strong mainly as social groups; yet Paul frames it as a matter of conscience and conviction, not ethnicity alone.

debates.bearing: Either way these are true believers whom God has welcomed; the point is that the strong must not despise them nor the weak condemn the strong, for both belong to the Lord.

debates.question: Does declaring all foods clean abolish every distinction of conscience?

views.label: Freedom is real but must be governed by love
views.case: The historic reading holds that Paul truly teaches all foods are clean, yet insists that knowledge must serve love; the strong are free, but not free to injure a weaker brother, so they gladly restrain their liberty.

views.label: The weak conscience should simply be corrected
views.case: Some would press the weak to grow up quickly into full freedom; but Paul does not command the weak to change their diet, he commands the strong to bear with them and not to make them stumble.

debates.bearing: The historic reading guards both truth and love: all foods are clean, yet love will forgo a lawful thing rather than destroy the work of God in a brother.

study.originalLanguages: Paul opens with proslambano, a warm word for welcoming a guest to your table and your heart, and applies it to the one who is asthenes, weak, tender in conscience over food and days. He twice forbids the church to krino, to pass condemning verdict on a brother, because there is one Judge before whom every knee will bow. He warns against setting a skandalon, the trigger of a snare, in a brother's path. And he lifts our eyes to the basileia, the kingdom of God, which is not eating and drinking but righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit, so that we measure our disputes by the Spirit's reign and not by the menu.

typology.fulfillment: The dietary laws that separated Israel pointed to a deeper holiness; Christ declared all foods clean, so the shadow yields to the substance in Him, Mark 7:19.
The sabbaths and feasts were a shadow of things to come; the reality belongs to Christ, so believers observe or abstain to the Lord, Colossians 2:16-17.
The oath of Isaiah 45:23 that every knee will bow to God is fulfilled in the universal lordship of the risen Christ before whose judgment seat we all stand.

study.christ: Christ stands at the center as Lord. Paul says Christ died and returned to life for this very purpose, that He might be Lord of both the dead and the living, so that whether we live or die we belong to Him. This lordship dissolves our right to judge, for the brother is the Lord's servant, not ours. And Christ's death sets the value of the brother we are tempted to wound: he is one for whom Christ died. To injure a weak believer over food is to disregard the price the Savior paid for him. Every appeal in the chapter runs back to the crucified and risen Lord who owns us all.
study.god: God is the one who welcomes, and He is the one who judges. Because God has accepted both the strong and the weak, no believer has standing to reject whom God receives. Because God alone is the Judge before whose seat we will all give account, no believer may usurp His bench. This frees the conscience from the tyranny of others' opinions and binds it instead to God alone. And God's kingdom is defined not by external rules but by righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit, the marks of His gracious reign in His people.

crossReferences.note: Jesus declares all foods clean, the ground of Paul's conviction that nothing is unclean in itself.
Paul refuses to let his liberty become a stumbling block that wounds a weak brother's conscience.
Let no one judge you over food, drink, or festivals, which were a shadow; the reality is Christ.
Every knee will bow and every tongue confess to God, the oath Paul cites to establish the coming judgment.

study.oneStory: The gospel that declared sinners righteous by faith now shapes a family in which strong and weak belong together under one Lord. The clean and unclean laws that once fenced Israel have reached their end in Christ, who makes all foods clean and welcomes all who trust Him. So the long story of God gathers a people from every background and conviction, each answering not to one another but to the Lord who died and rose to own them, moving toward the day when every knee bows before Him.

quiz.q: How does Paul say believers should treat the one whose faith is weak?

options.text: Welcome him without passing judgment on his opinions, because God has accepted him.
options.note: Paul commands a full welcome, since God has already welcomed him.

options.text: Tolerate him quietly while looking down on his scruples.
options.note: This at least stops short of excluding him, but Paul calls for genuine welcome, not condescension.

options.text: Exclude him until his faith becomes strong.
options.note: The command is to accept him now, not to place him on probation.

options.text: Require him to adopt the practice of the strong at once.
options.note: Paul does not command the weak to change; he commands the strong to bear with him.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say we must not judge a fellow believer in these matters?

options.text: Because he is the Lord's servant and we will all stand before God's judgment seat.
options.note: Each believer answers to his own Master, and God alone is the Judge.

options.text: Because right and wrong do not really exist in such matters.
options.note: Paul does not deny truth; he denies us the right to usurp God's bench.

options.text: Because everyone's opinion is equally valid on every subject.
options.note: The point is God's sole judgeship, not that all views are equally true.

options.text: Because judging others is simply impolite.
options.note: Judging here is far more than bad manners; it usurps the place of God, before whom each of us will give account.

quiz.q: What does Paul say the kingdom of God consists of?

options.text: Righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit, not eating and drinking.
options.note: Paul lifts the church above dietary disputes to the Spirit's reign.

options.text: Careful observance of food laws and holy days.
options.note: Paul says explicitly the kingdom is not a matter of eating and drinking.

options.text: Mainly outward acts of religious devotion.
options.note: The kingdom does involve devotion to God, but Paul locates it in righteousness, peace, and joy in the Spirit, not in outward observance.

options.text: Personal freedom to do whatever one pleases.
options.note: It is righteousness and peace in the Spirit, not unbounded self-will.

quiz.q: Why should the strong be willing to limit their freedom?

options.text: So they do not by their eating destroy a brother for whom Christ died.
options.note: Love will forgo a lawful thing rather than wound one Christ died for.

options.text: Because their freedom is not really genuine.
options.note: Their freedom is real, yet it must yield in love so as not to destroy a brother for whom Christ died.

options.text: Because food and drink are inherently sinful.
options.note: Paul says all food is clean; the issue is love for the brother.

options.text: So they can earn approval from the weak.
options.note: The motive is love for the brother's good, not winning approval.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean when he says whatever is not from faith is sin?

options.text: Acting against one's conscience, without the conviction of faith, is sin.
options.note: The doubter who eats is condemned because he does not act from faith.

options.text: Only unbelievers ever commit sin.
options.note: Paul is addressing believers and the way they act within their convictions.

options.text: Every ordinary action is automatically sinful.
options.note: The point is acting against a doubting conscience, not that all action is sin.

options.text: Faith makes any action whatsoever acceptable.
options.note: Acting from faith is essential, but this does not license sin; it means acting in the settled conviction that it pleases God.

questions: Paul commands us to welcome the one whose faith is weak, just as God has welcomed him. Who in your church do you find easy to look down on, and how would God's welcome reshape the way you treat them?
We will all stand before God's judgment seat and give account of ourselves. How does remembering that God alone is the Judge free you from both judging others and fearing their judgment of you?
Paul says the brother you might wound is one for whom Christ died. How does the cross change the value you place on a fellow believer you disagree with?
The kingdom of God is righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit. Where are you tempted to major on secondary matters and miss the heart of God's reign?
Whatever is not from faith is sin. Where is God calling you to act from settled conviction before Him rather than from mere pressure or doubt?

translationNotes.note: The phrase rendered without passing judgment on his opinions can also be translated without quarreling over disputable matters, as the BSB footnote notes; either way Paul forbids turning matters of conscience into occasions for dispute.
Some manuscripts add a clause about the one who does not regard the day; the sense is unchanged, that each believer acts to the Lord and gives thanks to God whether he observes a day or not.

Everything that is not from faith is sin speaks of acting against a doubting conscience; a few manuscripts place the closing doxology of Romans 16:25-27 after this verse, but the standard text keeps it at the end of the letter.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 14:1-23. Poetic quotation lines in verse 11 were concatenated into the running verse text with single spaces. No em dashes required substitution. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
